lessons.title: Pointers and references to the base class of derived objects

exercises.title: Base reference calls Base version
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate that calling a member function through a `Base&` reference invokes the base-class version, even when the actual object is a derived type.

## Input

- A single line containing a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Direct: <name> says Woof`
2. `Via ref: <name> says ...`
3. `Via ptr: <name> says ...`

where `<name>` is the name read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a `std::string` member `m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a public `const std::string& getName() const` accessor, and a public `std::string_view speak() const` that returns `"..."`.
- Define a class `Dog` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. `Dog`'s constructor takes `const std::string&` and forwards it to `Animal`. `Dog` has its own `std::string_view speak() const` that returns `"Woof"`.
- In `main`, read the name and create a `Dog` object.
- Print `Direct: <name> says <speak>` by calling `speak()` directly on the `Dog` object.
- Create a `const Animal&` reference to the `Dog` and print `Via ref: <name> says <speak>` using the reference.
- Create a `const Animal*` pointer to the `Dog` and print `Via ptr: <name> says <speak>` using the pointer.
- The reference and pointer calls must demonstrate that the `Animal` version of `speak()` is invoked.

test_cases.label: Dog named Rex
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Rex says Woof
Via ref: Rex says ...
Via ptr: Rex says ...

test_cases.label: Dog named Buddy
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Buddy says Woof
Via ref: Buddy says ...
Via ptr: Buddy says ...

test_cases.label: Dog named Max
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Max says Woof
Via ref: Max says ...
Via ptr: Max says ...

exercises.title: Function taking Base& hides derived behavior
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Write a `report` function that accepts a `const Shape&` and prints the shape's description. Show that when a `Circle` is passed, the base `Shape` description is printed instead of the `Circle` description, because the function parameter is a base reference.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `shapeName` (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Line 2: a double `radius` (0.1 to 1000.0).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Circle: <shapeName> r=<radius>` (direct call on the Circle)
2. `Report: <shapeName>: generic shape` (via the report function taking const Shape&)
3. `Direct: <shapeName> r=<radius>` (direct call on Circle again, proving the object itself is fine)

The radius is printed with default `std::cout` formatting (no fixed precision).

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a `std::string` member `m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a public `const std::string& getName() const` accessor, and a public `std::string describe() const` that returns `"<name>: generic shape"`.
- Define a class `Circle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It has an additional `double m_radius` member. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and `double`. It has its own `std::string describe() const` that returns `"<name> r=<radius>"` (use `std::to_string` for the radius).
- Define a free function `void report(const Shape& s)` that prints `Report: ` followed by the result of calling `describe()` on `s`.
- In `main`, read the name and radius, create a `Circle`, print `Circle: ` followed by calling `describe()` directly on the circle, then call `report(circle)`, then print `Direct: ` followed by calling `describe()` directly on the circle again.

test_cases.label: Disk with radius 5
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Disk r=5.000000
Report: Disk: generic shape
Direct: Disk r=5.000000

test_cases.label: Ring with radius 2.5
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Ring r=2.500000
Report: Ring: generic shape
Direct: Ring r=2.500000

test_cases.label: Wheel with radius 10
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: Wheel r=10.000000
Report: Wheel: generic shape
Direct: Wheel r=10.000000

lessons.title: Virtual functions and polymorphism

exercises.title: Virtual speak
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Complete a small class hierarchy where a `virtual` function in the base class enables the correct derived version to be called through a base-class reference.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) -- the number of animals.
- Next `N` lines: a single word, either `cat` or `dog`.

## Output

For each animal, print the result of calling `speak()` through an `Animal` reference:
- A `Cat` speaks `Meow`
- A `Dog` speaks `Woof`

Each on its own line.

## Requirements

- Define a base class `Animal` with a `virtual` member function `std::string_view speak() const` that returns `"..."`.
- Define `Cat` (publicly derived from `Animal`) whose `speak()` returns `"Meow"`.
- Define `Dog` (publicly derived from `Animal`) whose `speak()` returns `"Woof"`.
- In `main`, read each animal type, create the appropriate object, and call a free function `void printSpeak(const Animal& a)` that prints `a.speak()` followed by a newline.
- You must call `speak()` through the `Animal&` parameter, not directly on the derived type.

exercises.title: Polymorphic shape areas
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a polymorphic shape hierarchy where `Circle` and `Rectangle` are derived from `Shape`. Store different shapes in a `std::vector<Shape*>` and compute the total area through virtual dispatch -- the classic polymorphism pattern.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20) -- the number of shapes.
- Next `N` lines: either `circle <radius>` or `rect <width> <height>`, where all values are positive integers (1 <= value <= 100).

## Output

- One line: the total area of all shapes, printed as a **truncated integer** (use `static_cast<int>(totalArea)`).

Use `pi = 3.14159265` (provided as a constant) for circle area.

## Requirements

- Define a base class `Shape` with a `virtual double area() const` member function that returns `0.0`. Include a virtual destructor (`virtual ~Shape() = default;`) since derived objects will be deleted through base pointers.
- Define `Circle` (derived from `Shape`) with a private `int m_radius` member. Its `area()` returns `pi * m_radius * m_radius`.
- Define `Rectangle` (derived from `Shape`) with private `int m_width` and `int m_height` members. Its `area()` returns `m_width * m_height` (as a `double`).
- In `main`, read each shape into a `std::vector<Shape*>` (you may use `new` to allocate, or use local arrays -- but the vector must hold base pointers).
- Loop over the vector calling `area()` through the `Shape*` to accumulate the total.
- Print `static_cast<int>(total)` followed by a newline.
- Clean up any dynamically allocated memory with `delete`.

lessons.title: The override and final specifiers, and covariant return types

exercises.title: Override specifier catches mismatches
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Use the `override` specifier to correctly override a base class virtual function. Create a class hierarchy where `Animal` provides a virtual `speak()` method and `Cat` overrides it.

## Input

- A single line containing an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 3), the number of cats to create.

## Output

For each cat, print one line:
```
Cat says: Meow
```

Followed by a final line:
```
Animal says: ...
```

For input `2`:
```
Cat says: Meow
Cat says: Meow
Animal says: ...
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a public virtual function `std::string speak() const` that returns `"Animal says: ..."`. Include a virtual destructor (`virtual ~Animal() = default;`) since derived objects will be deleted through base pointers.
- Define a class `Cat` that publicly inherits from `Animal` and overrides `speak()` using the `override` specifier. It returns `"Cat says: Meow"`.
- In `main`, read `n`. Create `n` `Cat` objects stored via `Animal*` pointers (in a fixed-size array of 3 `Animal*` initialized to `nullptr`). Call `speak()` through each pointer and print the result.
- After printing all cats, create one plain `Animal` on the stack, call `speak()`, and print its result.
- Delete all dynamically allocated cats before the program ends.

test_cases.label: Two cats then one base
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cat says: Meow
Cat says: Meow
Animal says: ...

test_cases.label: Three cats then one base
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cat says: Meow
Cat says: Meow
Cat says: Meow
Animal says: ...

exercises.title: Covariant return types with final
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a `Shape` hierarchy that uses covariant return types and the `final` specifier. A base `clone()` function returns `Shape*`, while each derived override returns its own pointer type. The `Circle` class marks `clone()` as `final` so no further derived class can override it.

## Input

- Line 1: a string, either `circle` or `square`.
- Line 2: a double `value` representing the radius (for circle) or side length (for square).

## Output

Two lines. The first describes the original object and the second describes the cloned object. Both lines must be identical, proving the clone matches the original.

For input `circle` / `3.5`:
```
Circle r=3.5
Circle r=3.5
```

For input `square` / `4`:
```
Square s=4.0
Square s=4.0
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a public virtual destructor (`virtual ~Shape() = default;`), a public virtual function `Shape* clone() const` that returns `new Shape{*this}`, and a public virtual function `std::string describe() const` that returns `"Shape"`.
- Define a class `Circle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It stores a `double m_radius`. Override `clone()` with a covariant return type (`Circle*`) and mark it `override final`. Override `describe()` with `override` to return `"Circle r=<radius>"`. Use a fixed precision of one decimal place for the radius (use `std::ostringstream` with `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)`, or any approach that produces one decimal digit).
- Define a class `Square` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It stores a `double m_side`. Override `clone()` with covariant return type (`Square*`) and `override`. Override `describe()` with `override` to return `"Square s=<side>"` with one decimal place.
- In `main`, read the shape type and value. Create the appropriate derived object via `new`. Call `describe()` through a `Shape*` and print it. Then call `clone()`, print `describe()` on the clone, and `delete` both objects.

test_cases.label: Clone a circle with integer radius

lessons.title: Virtual destructors, virtual assignment, and overriding virtualization

exercises.title: Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate that a virtual destructor causes both the derived and base destructors to run when deleting through a base pointer. Print from each destructor to prove the correct destruction order.

## Input

- A single line containing an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 3), the number of `Derived` objects to create and destroy.

## Output

For each object, two lines are printed when it is deleted (derived destructor first, then base):
```
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
```

After all deletions, print a final line:
```
Done
```

For input `2`:
```
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Done
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with a **virtual** destructor that prints `Destroying Base` followed by a newline.
- Define a class `Derived` that publicly inherits from `Base`. Its destructor uses `override` and prints `Destroying Derived` followed by a newline.
- In `main`, read `n`. Use a loop to create `n` `Derived` objects via `new`, each stored in a `Base*` pointer. Immediately after creating each object, `delete` it through the base pointer (create one, delete one, then loop).
- After the loop, print `Done`.

test_cases.label: Two objects destroyed properly
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Done

test_cases.label: Three objects
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Destroying Derived
Destroying Base
Done

exercises.title: Resource-owning hierarchy with virtual destructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a two-level class hierarchy where the derived class owns a dynamically allocated resource. Use a virtual destructor so that deleting through a base pointer correctly cleans up the resource. Print lifecycle messages from constructors and destructors to prove the correct order.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `label` (1-20 characters, no spaces) naming the resource.
- Line 2: an integer `size` (1 <= size <= 100) representing a resource size.

## Output

The program creates a `Resource` (derived) via a `Base*`, uses it, then deletes it. The exact output for input `Buffer` / `64`:
```
Base created
Resource acquired: Buffer size 64
Using: Buffer size 64
Resource released: Buffer size 64
Base destroyed
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with a virtual destructor. The constructor prints `Base created` and the destructor prints `Base destroyed` (each followed by a newline).
- Define a class `Resource` that publicly inherits from `Base`. It stores a `std::string m_label` and an `int m_size`. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and `int`, calls the `Base` constructor, stores the values, and prints `Resource acquired: <label> size <size>`. Its destructor uses `override` and prints `Resource released: <label> size <size>`.
- `Resource` has a public `void use() const` method that prints `Using: <label> size <size>`.
- In `main`, read the label and size. Create a `Resource` via `new` and store the result in a `Base*` pointer. Cast the pointer back to `Resource*` using `static_cast` to call `use()`. Then `delete` the base pointer.
- Do **not** use `dynamic_cast` (that topic has not been covered yet).

test_cases.label: Buffer resource size 64
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base created
Resource acquired: Buffer size 64
Using: Buffer size 64
Resource released: Buffer size 64
Base destroyed

test_cases.label: Cache resource size 256
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base created
Resource acquired: Cache size 256
Using: Cache size 256
Resource released: Cache size 256
Base destroyed

test_cases.label: Pool resource size 1
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base created
Resource acquired: Pool size 1
Using: Pool size 1
Resource released: Pool size 1
Base destroyed

lessons.title: Early binding and late binding

exercises.title: Observe early vs late binding
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate the behavioral difference between early binding and late binding. A non-virtual function is resolved at compile time (early binding), while a virtual function is resolved at runtime (late binding). Both functions exist on the same class hierarchy.

## Input

- A single integer `n` (1 <= n <= 5), the number of objects to create.

## Output

For each object, print two lines:
1. `Early: Base` (the non-virtual call through `Base&` always resolves to `Base`)
2. `Late: Derived` (the virtual call through `Base&` resolves to `Derived` at runtime)

After all objects, print one final line:
```
Done
```

For input `2`:
```
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Done
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with two public const member functions:
  - `std::string_view earlyBound() const` (non-virtual) that returns `"Base"`.
  - `virtual std::string_view lateBound() const` that returns `"Base"`.
- Define a class `Derived` that publicly inherits from `Base` with:
  - `std::string_view earlyBound() const` (non-virtual, hides the base version) that returns `"Derived"`.
  - `std::string_view lateBound() const override` that returns `"Derived"`.
- In `main`, read `n`. For each iteration, create a `Derived` object on the stack. Create a `const Base&` reference to it. Print `Early: ` followed by calling `earlyBound()` through the reference. Print `Late: ` followed by calling `lateBound()` through the reference.
- After the loop, print `Done`.

test_cases.label: Two objects
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Done

test_cases.label: Five objects
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Early: Base
Late: Derived
Done

exercises.title: Polymorphic dispatch with mixed types
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a small hierarchy with `Instrument` as the base class and two derived classes, `Piano` and `Drum`. Store mixed instruments in an array of base pointers and observe that virtual calls (late binding) dispatch to the correct derived function while non-virtual calls (early binding) always call the base version.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 6), the number of instruments.
- Next `N` lines: a single word, either `piano` or `drum`.

## Output

For each instrument, print two lines:
1. `Binding: static` (the non-virtual `bindingType()` through `Instrument*` always returns the base version)
2. `Sound: <sound>` (the virtual `play()` through `Instrument*` returns the derived version)

where `<sound>` is `Plink` for piano and `Boom` for drum.

After all instruments, print:
```
Total: <N>
```

For input `3` / `piano` / `drum` / `piano`:
```
Binding: static
Sound: Plink
Binding: static
Sound: Boom
Binding: static
Sound: Plink
Total: 3
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Instrument` with:
  - A non-virtual public function `std::string_view bindingType() const` that returns `"static"`.
  - A `virtual` public function `std::string_view play() const` that returns `"..."`. 
  - A `virtual` destructor (use `virtual ~Instrument() = default;`).
- Define `Piano` (publicly derived from `Instrument`) that overrides `play()` to return `"Plink"`. It also has its own non-virtual `bindingType()` that returns `"dynamic"` (this version will never be called through the base pointer, proving early binding).
- Define `Drum` (publicly derived from `Instrument`) that overrides `play()` to return `"Boom"`. It also has its own non-virtual `bindingType()` that returns `"dynamic"`.
- In `main`, read `N`. Use a `std::vector<Instrument*>` to store `N` dynamically allocated instruments. Loop over the vector, calling `bindingType()` and `play()` through the `Instrument*`. Print `Total: <N>`. Delete all instruments.

test_cases.label: Mixed piano and drum
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Binding: static
Sound: Plink
Binding: static
Sound: Boom
Binding: static
Sound: Plink
Total: 3

test_cases.label: All drums
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Binding: static
Sound: Boom
Binding: static
Sound: Boom
Total: 2

lessons.title: The virtual table

exercises.title: Measuring the vptr overhead
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate that adding virtual functions to a class increases its `sizeof` because of the hidden vptr. Build several structs with and without virtual functions and report their sizes.

**Note:** The expected sizes below assume a 64-bit platform where `sizeof(int)` is 4 and `sizeof(void*)` is 8. Our Judge0 runtime uses x86-64 Linux, so these values will be correct there.

## Input

No input. The program ignores `stdin`.

## Output

Exactly four lines:

```
Plain: 4
OneVirtual: 16
TwoVirtuals: 16
Derived: 16
```

Each line prints the name of the struct followed by its `sizeof`.

## Requirements

- Define a struct `Plain` with a single `int m_value{}` member and **no** virtual functions.
- Define a struct `OneVirtual` with a single `int m_value{}` member and **one** virtual function `virtual void f() {}`.
- Define a struct `TwoVirtuals` with a single `int m_value{}` member and **two** virtual functions `virtual void f() {}` and `virtual void g() {}`. This demonstrates that adding more virtual functions does not increase object size -- only one vptr is stored regardless of the number of virtual functions.
- Define a struct `Derived` that publicly inherits from `OneVirtual` and overrides `f()` with `void f() override {}`. It adds no new data members. This demonstrates that the derived class still has only one vptr (inherited from the base).
- In `main`, print the four lines shown above using `sizeof` on each struct.
- The program takes no input.

exercises.title: Vtable dispatch in action
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a class hierarchy that demonstrates virtual dispatch through base pointers. Create a `Base` class and two derived classes. Store objects through `Base*` pointers and show that the correct override is called at runtime -- the observable effect of the vtable.

**Note:** The expected `sizeof` values assume a 64-bit platform where `sizeof(void*)` is 8 and `sizeof(double)` is 8. Our Judge0 runtime uses x86-64 Linux, so these values will be correct there.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10), the number of objects to create.
- Next `N` lines: each line is either `base`, `alpha`, or `beta`.

## Output

For each object, print two values on one line separated by a space: the result of calling `identify()` through a `Base*`, and the `sizeof` the pointed-to object (obtained by dereferencing the pointer and using `sizeof`).

For input:
```
3
base
alpha
beta
```

Output:
```
Base 8
Alpha 8
Beta 16
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with no data members and a `virtual std::string identify() const` that returns `"Base"`. Because `Base` has a virtual function, its only content is the hidden vptr.
- Define a class `Alpha` that publicly inherits from `Base` and overrides `identify()` to return `"Alpha"`. It adds no new data members, so its size equals `Base`.
- Define a class `Beta` that publicly inherits from `Base`, adds a `double m_value{}` data member, and overrides `identify()` to return `"Beta"`. The extra `double` increases its size beyond `Base`.
- In `main`, read `N` and then read each type string. For each type, dynamically allocate the correct derived object (or `Base` for `"base"`) and store it in a `Base*`.
- For each pointer, print `identify()` followed by a space and then `sizeof` the dereferenced object. Note: `sizeof(*ptr)` uses the static type (`Base`), so you must use `sizeof(Base)`, `sizeof(Alpha)`, or `sizeof(Beta)` based on the type string you read. This mirrors what you would see if you inspected each object's actual memory footprint.
- Delete all allocated objects before the program ends.
- Use `virtual` destructors where appropriate.

lessons.title: Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

exercises.title: Shape area via abstract base class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a `Shape` abstract base class with a pure virtual `area()` function, then implement two concrete derived classes (`Circle` and `Rectangle`) that override it. Demonstrate polymorphism by calling `area()` through base-class pointers.

## Input

- Line 1: a string, either `circle` or `rectangle`.
- Line 2: if `circle`, a single double `r` (the radius). If `rectangle`, two doubles `w` and `h` (width and height), space-separated.

## Output

A single line: `Area: ` followed by the computed area with exactly 2 decimal places.

For input `circle` / `5.0`:
```
Area: 78.54
```

For input `rectangle` / `3.0 4.0`:
```
Area: 12.00
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a public pure virtual function `double area() const = 0` and a public `virtual ~Shape() = default`.
- Define a class `Circle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It stores a `double m_radius` and overrides `area()` using `override`. Compute area as `3.14159265 * r * r`.
- Define a class `Rectangle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`. It stores `double m_width` and `double m_height` and overrides `area()` using `override`. Compute area as `w * h`.
- In `main`, read the shape type and its parameters. Create the appropriate derived object with `new`, store it in a `Shape*`, print the area formatted to 2 decimal places using `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)`, then `delete` the object.
- You must call `area()` through the `Shape*` pointer, not directly on the derived type.

exercises.title: Interface class for printable objects
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Define an **interface class** `IPrintable` with a single pure virtual function, then implement it in two unrelated classes. Demonstrate that both can be used polymorphically through the interface.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 5), the number of objects.
- Next `N` lines: each line starts with a word (`book` or `invoice`), followed by parameters:
  - `book <title>` -- a single-word title (no spaces).
  - `invoice <amount>` -- a double representing the total.

## Output

For each object, print the result of calling `print()` through an `IPrintable*`:
- A `Book` prints: `Book: <title>`
- An `Invoice` prints: `Invoice: $<amount>` with exactly 2 decimal places.

For input:
```
3
book Algorithms
invoice 49.99
book Compilers
```
Output:
```
Book: Algorithms
Invoice: $49.99
Book: Compilers
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `IPrintable` with a public pure virtual function `std::string getDescription() const = 0` and a public `virtual ~IPrintable() = default`. It must have no member variables and no other functions -- this is an interface class.
- Define a class `Book` that publicly inherits from `IPrintable`. It stores a `std::string m_title` and overrides `getDescription()` with `override` to return `"Book: <title>"`.
- Define a class `Invoice` that publicly inherits from `IPrintable`. It stores a `double m_amount` and overrides `getDescription()` with `override` to return `"Invoice: $<amount>"` with 2 decimal places.
- In `main`, read `N` objects. For each, create the appropriate derived object with `new`, store it in an `IPrintable*`, print `getDescription()`, and `delete` it.

lessons.title: Virtual base classes

exercises.title: Solving the diamond with virtual inheritance
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Convert a diamond hierarchy from regular inheritance to virtual inheritance so that the most-derived class contains only one copy of the shared base.

## Input

- Line 1: a single integer `value` (-10000 <= value <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `value: <value>` -- printed by accessing `m_value` directly (no disambiguation needed).
- Line 2: `B greet: hello <value>`
- Line 3: `C greet: hello <value>`

## Requirements

- Define a class `A` with a public `int m_value{}` member and a constructor that takes an `int` and sets `m_value`.
- Define a class `B` that **virtually** inherits from `A` (`class B : virtual public A`). It has a constructor taking an `int` that forwards to `A`. It has a public method `void greet() const` that prints `B greet: hello ` followed by `m_value` and a newline.
- Define a class `C` that **virtually** inherits from `A` (`class C : virtual public A`). It has a constructor taking an `int` that forwards to `A`. It has a public method `void greet() const` that prints `C greet: hello ` followed by `m_value` and a newline.
- Define a class `D` that publicly inherits from both `B` and `C`. Its constructor takes a single `int` and initializes `A`, `B`, and `C` with that value. The most-derived class `D` must directly initialize `A` in its member initializer list.
- In `main`, read the integer, construct a `D`, print `value: ` followed by `d.m_value` and a newline, then call `d.greet()` for `B` and `d.greet()` for `C` (use `d.B::greet()` and `d.C::greet()`).

test_cases.label: Positive value
test_cases.expected_stdout:
value: 42
B greet: hello 42
C greet: hello 42

test_cases.label: Negative value
test_cases.expected_stdout:
value: -7
B greet: hello -7
C greet: hello -7

test_cases.label: Zero value
test_cases.expected_stdout:
value: 0
B greet: hello 0
C greet: hello 0

exercises.title: Constructor responsibility in virtual inheritance
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a deeper diamond hierarchy and demonstrate that the most-derived class controls how the virtual base is initialized, even when intermediate classes specify different values.

## Input

- Line 1: three integers `bVal`, `cVal`, and `dVal` (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `A::m_value: <dVal>` -- proving that `D`'s initialization of `A` wins.
- Line 2: `B::m_bonus: <bVal>`
- Line 3: `C::m_bonus: <cVal>`
- Line 4: `total: <dVal + bVal + cVal>`

## Requirements

- Define a class `A` with a public `int m_value{}` member and a constructor taking an `int`.
- Define a class `B` that **virtually** inherits from `A`. `B` has its own public `int m_bonus{}` member. Its constructor takes two ints `(aVal, bonus)`: it forwards `aVal` to `A` and sets `m_bonus` to `bonus`.
- Define a class `C` that **virtually** inherits from `A`. `C` has its own public `int m_bonus{}` member. Its constructor takes two ints `(aVal, bonus)`: it forwards `aVal` to `A` and sets `m_bonus` to `bonus`.
- Define a class `D` that publicly inherits from both `B` and `C`. Its constructor takes three ints `(bVal, cVal, dVal)`. It must directly initialize `A` with `dVal`, initialize `B` with `(0, bVal)`, and initialize `C` with `(0, cVal)`. Note that `B`'s and `C`'s `aVal` arguments are ignored for the virtual base -- `D` controls `A`.
- `D` has a public method `void show() const` that prints the four output lines described above.
- In `main`, read three integers, construct a `D`, and call `show()`.

lessons.title: Object slicing

exercises.title: Spot the slice
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate object slicing by showing the difference between calling a virtual function through a by-value copy versus through a reference.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).

## Output

Exactly four lines:
1. `Direct: <name> is a Cat`
2. `Ref: <name> is a Cat`
3. `Sliced: <name> is an Animal`
4. `Legs after slice: 0`

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a `std::string m_name` member, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a public `const std::string& getName() const` accessor, and a public `virtual std::string describe() const` that returns `getName() + " is an Animal"`.
- Define a class `Cat` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. It has an additional `int m_legs{4}` member. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and forwards it to `Animal`. Override `describe()` (use `override`) to return `getName() + " is a Cat"`. Add a public `int getLegs() const` that returns `m_legs`.
- In `main`, read the name and create a `Cat` object.
- Print `Direct: ` followed by calling `describe()` directly on the `Cat`.
- Create a `const Animal&` reference to the `Cat` and print `Ref: ` followed by calling `describe()` through the reference.
- Create an `Animal` variable (by value) initialized from the `Cat`. Print `Sliced: ` followed by calling `describe()` on this sliced copy.
- Print `Legs after slice: 0` (since the sliced `Animal` has no `m_legs` member, the derived data is gone -- print the literal `0`).

test_cases.label: Cat named Whiskers
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Whiskers is a Cat
Ref: Whiskers is a Cat
Sliced: Whiskers is an Animal
Legs after slice: 0

test_cases.label: Cat named Luna
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Luna is a Cat
Ref: Luna is a Cat
Sliced: Luna is an Animal
Legs after slice: 0

test_cases.label: Cat named Milo
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Direct: Milo is a Cat
Ref: Milo is a Cat
Sliced: Milo is an Animal
Legs after slice: 0

exercises.title: Fix the slicing bug
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

You are given a program that stores shapes in a `std::vector` and prints their areas. The original code uses `std::vector<Shape>`, which causes object slicing -- every derived shape is sliced to a plain `Shape` with area `0`. Fix the code to use `std::vector<Shape*>` so virtual dispatch works correctly.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) -- the number of shapes.
- Next `N` lines: either `circle <radius>` or `rect <width> <height>`, where all values are positive integers (1 <= value <= 100).

## Output

For each shape, print one line:
- `Circle: <area>` for circles
- `Rect: <area>` for rectangles

where `<area>` is the area as a **truncated integer** (`static_cast<int>(area)`).

Use `pi = 3.14159265` for circle area.

## Requirements

- A base class `Shape` with a virtual destructor (`virtual ~Shape() = default;`), `virtual std::string label() const` returning `"Shape"`, and `virtual double area() const` returning `0.0`.
- A `Circle` class (derived from `Shape`) with `int m_radius`. Override `label()` to return `"Circle"` and `area()` to return `pi * m_radius * m_radius`. Both use `override`.
- A `Rectangle` class (derived from `Shape`) with `int m_width` and `int m_height`. Override `label()` to return `"Rect"` and `area()` to return `static_cast<double>(m_width) * m_height`. Both use `override`.
- Store shapes in a `std::vector<Shape*>` (allocate with `new`).
- Loop over the vector printing `<label>: <truncated_area>` for each shape.
- Delete all dynamically allocated shapes before the program ends.
- Do NOT use `std::vector<Shape>` -- that causes slicing and is the bug you are fixing.

lessons.title: Dynamic casting

exercises.title: Safe downcast with dynamic_cast
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Use `dynamic_cast<Derived*>` to safely determine the actual type of objects accessed through base pointers, and call a derived-only function when the cast succeeds.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) -- the number of objects.
- Next `N` lines: either `base` or `derived`.

## Output

For each object, print one line:
- If the object is a `Base`, the `dynamic_cast` to `Derived*` fails. Print: `Cast failed: base object`
- If the object is a `Derived`, the cast succeeds. Print: `Cast succeeded: <message>` where `<message>` is the return value of `Derived::special()`.

`Derived::special()` returns `"I am Derived"`.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with a virtual destructor (use `= default`).
- Define a class `Derived` that publicly inherits from `Base`. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string special() const` that returns `"I am Derived"`.
- In `main`, read `N`, then for each line create either a `Base` or `Derived` on the heap (via `new`), storing the result in a `Base*`.
- For each `Base*`, use `dynamic_cast<Derived*>` to attempt a downcast. If the result is not `nullptr`, print `Cast succeeded: ` followed by calling `special()` on the resulting pointer. Otherwise print `Cast failed: base object`.
- Delete each object after processing it.

test_cases.label: Mixed base and derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast failed: base object
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast failed: base object

test_cases.label: All derived
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cast succeeded: I am Derived
Cast succeeded: I am Derived

exercises.title: Counting derived types in a polymorphic collection
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a three-level hierarchy (`Animal` -> `Dog`, `Animal` -> `Cat`) and use `dynamic_cast` to count how many objects in a polymorphic collection are of a specific derived type. Then call a derived-only function on each successfully cast object.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20) -- the number of animals.
- Next `N` lines: either `dog <name>` or `cat <name>`, where `<name>` is a single word (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Last line: a string, either `dog` or `cat`, indicating which type to search for.

## Output

- For each animal that matches the search type (via `dynamic_cast`), print the result of calling the type-specific function:
  - For `Dog`: `Dog: <name> says Woof`
  - For `Cat`: `Cat: <name> says Meow`
- After all matches, print: `Found: <count>` where `<count>` is the number of successful casts.

If no animals match, print only `Found: 0`.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a `std::string m_name` member, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a virtual destructor (`= default`), and a public accessor `const std::string& getName() const`.
- Define a class `Dog` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. Its constructor forwards the name. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string bark() const` that returns `"Dog: " + getName() + " says Woof"`.
- Define a class `Cat` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. Its constructor forwards the name. Add a non-virtual member function `std::string meow() const` that returns `"Cat: " + getName() + " says Meow"`.
- In `main`, read all animals into a `std::vector<Animal*>` (allocated with `new`).
- Read the search type. Loop over the vector and use `dynamic_cast` to attempt casting each `Animal*` to the target type. On success, call the appropriate function (`bark()` or `meow()`) and print the result. Count successes.
- Print `Found: <count>` at the end.
- Delete all animals before exiting.

test_cases.label: Find dogs in mixed collection
test_cases.stdin:
4
dog Rex
cat Whiskers
dog Buddy
cat Luna
dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dog: Rex says Woof
Dog: Buddy says Woof
Found: 2

test_cases.label: Find cats in all-cat collection
test_cases.stdin:
3
cat Milo
cat Nala
cat Simba
cat
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Cat: Milo says Meow
Cat: Nala says Meow
Cat: Simba says Meow
Found: 3

lessons.title: Printing inherited classes using operator<<

exercises.title: Polymorphic printing via virtual print
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement the virtual `print` pattern so that `operator<<` produces the correct output for both `Shape` and `Circle` objects when printed through a `Shape&` reference.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Line 2: a double `radius`.

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `[Shape: <name>]`
2. `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`
3. `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`

The radius is printed with default `std::cout` formatting (no fixed precision).

Line 1 prints a `Shape` directly. Line 2 prints a `Circle` directly. Line 3 prints the same `Circle` through a `const Shape&` reference, demonstrating polymorphic dispatch.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a protected `std::string m_name` member and a constructor taking `const std::string&`.
- `Shape` has a public `virtual void print(std::ostream& out) const` that writes `[Shape: <name>]` to `out`.
- `Shape` has a `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Shape& s)` that calls `s.print(out)` and returns `out`.
- `Shape` has a `virtual ~Shape() = default`.
- Define `Circle` publicly derived from `Shape` with a `double m_radius` member. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and `double`.
- `Circle` overrides `print` (use `override`) to write `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`.
- In `main`, read the name and radius. Create a `Shape`, print it. Create a `Circle`, print it directly, then print it through a `const Shape&` reference.

exercises.title: Polymorphic employee directory
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build an `Employee` hierarchy where `Manager` and `Engineer` override a virtual `print` function. Store mixed employees in a `std::vector<Employee*>` and print them all through `operator<<`, demonstrating that the correct derived output appears without any per-class `operator<<` overloads.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10).
- Next `N` lines: either `manager <name> <dept>` or `engineer <name> <lang>`, where `<name>`, `<dept>`, and `<lang>` are single words (no spaces).

## Output

For each employee, one line:
- Manager: `Manager: <name>, dept=<dept>`
- Engineer: `Engineer: <name>, lang=<lang>`

Followed by a final line:
```
Total: <N>
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Employee` with a protected `std::string m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a `virtual ~Employee() = default`, a `virtual void print(std::ostream& out) const` that writes `Employee: <name>`, and a `friend operator<<` that calls `print`.
- Define `Manager` (publicly derived from `Employee`) with a `std::string m_dept` member. Constructor takes name and dept. Override `print` (use `override`) to write `Manager: <name>, dept=<dept>`.
- Define `Engineer` (publicly derived from `Employee`) with a `std::string m_lang` member. Constructor takes name and lang. Override `print` to write `Engineer: <name>, lang=<lang>`.
- In `main`, read N employees into a `std::vector<Employee*>` using `new`.
- Loop over the vector printing each employee with `std::cout << *ptr << '\n'`.
- Print `Total: <N>`.
- Delete all dynamically allocated employees.

test_cases.label: Mixed manager and engineer
test_cases.stdin:
3
manager Alice Sales
engineer Bob C++
manager Carol HR
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Manager: Alice, dept=Sales
Engineer: Bob, lang=C++
Manager: Carol, dept=HR
Total: 3

test_cases.label: All engineers
test_cases.stdin:
2
engineer Dave Python
engineer Eve Rust
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Engineer: Dave, lang=Python
Engineer: Eve, lang=Rust
Total: 2

lessons.title: Chapter 25 summary and quiz
lessons.summary_md:
## Chapter 25 Summary

This chapter covered virtual functions and the runtime polymorphism system that makes inheritance powerful in C++.

### Base pointers and the wrong version (25.1)

Calling a member function through a `Base*` or `Base&` resolves based on the static type, not the actual object type. The compiler picks the base version at compile time, ignoring any derived override.

### Virtual functions and polymorphism (25.2)

Marking a base function `virtual` defers resolution to runtime. The actual object type determines which version runs, enabling polymorphism through a single base reference.

### override, final, covariant returns (25.3)

`override` makes the compiler verify the function actually overrides a base virtual, catching signature mismatches. `final` prevents further overriding or inheritance. Covariant return types let an override return a more-derived pointer or reference.

### Virtual destructors (25.4)

Deleting a derived object through a base pointer without a virtual destructor skips the derived destructor, leaking resources. Any polymorphic base class needs a virtual destructor.

### Early vs late binding (25.5)

Early binding resolves calls at compile time (fast, inflexible). Late binding defers to runtime via virtual dispatch, adding one indirection per call.

### The virtual table (25.6)

Each polymorphic class has a vtable -- a static array of function pointers. Each object stores a hidden vptr pointing to its class's vtable. Virtual calls go through vptr to vtable to function pointer. Cost: one pointer per object, one indirection per call.

### Pure virtuals, abstract classes, interfaces (25.7)

A pure virtual function (`= 0`) forces derived classes to provide an implementation. A class with any pure virtual is abstract and cannot be instantiated. An interface class has only pure virtuals and a virtual destructor.

### Virtual base classes (25.8)

The diamond problem gives a class two copies of a shared ancestor. Declaring the shared base `virtual` in intermediate classes ensures one copy. The most-derived class constructs the virtual base.

### Object slicing (25.9)

Assigning a derived object to a base variable by value copies only the base portion, losing derived data and polymorphic behavior. Use pointers or references to avoid slicing.

### Dynamic casting (25.10)

`dynamic_cast<Derived*>(basePtr)` safely checks the runtime type, returning `nullptr` on failure (or throwing `std::bad_cast` for references). Requires at least one virtual function.

### Printing inherited classes (25.11)

Since `operator<<` cannot be virtual, the pattern is to have it call a virtual `print(std::ostream&)` member. Each derived class overrides `print`, and the single `operator<<` dispatches polymorphically.

exercises.title: Polymorphic Shape Container with Virtual Destructors
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build an abstract `Shape` base class with pure virtual `area()` and a virtual `print()` method. Derive `Circle` and `Rectangle`, store them polymorphically in a `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Shape>>`, and print each shape using `operator<<`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer N (1 <= N <= 20), the number of shapes.
- Next N lines: either `circle <radius>` or `rect <width> <height>`, where radius, width, and height are positive doubles.

## Output

For each shape in input order, print one line using `operator<<`:
- For a circle: `Circle: r=<radius> area=<area>`
- For a rectangle: `Rect: <width>x<height> area=<area>`

Then print a final line: `Total area: <sum>`

All floating-point values are printed with exactly 2 decimal places.

## Requirements

1. Abstract class `Shape` with: `virtual ~Shape() = default;`, pure virtual `double area() const = 0;`, virtual `void print(std::ostream& out) const = 0;`.
2. Free function `std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Shape& s)` that calls `s.print(out)` and returns `out`.
3. `Circle` derives from `Shape`, stores `double m_radius`. Overrides `area()` to return `pi * r * r` (use `M_PI` or `3.14159265358979323846`). Overrides `print` to output `Circle: r=<radius> area=<area>`.
4. `Rectangle` derives from `Shape`, stores `double m_width` and `double m_height`. Overrides `area()` to return `w * h`. Overrides `print` to output `Rect: <width>x<height> area=<area>`.
5. Use `override` on every derived virtual function.
6. In `main`, read shapes into `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Shape>>`, print each with `std::cout << *shape << '\n'`, then print total area.
7. Use `<iostream>`, `<memory>`, `<vector>`, `<string>`, `<iomanip>`, `<cmath>`. Set `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)` once at the start of `main`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- mixed shapes
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: r=5.00 area=78.54
Rect: 4.00x6.00 area=24.00
Circle: r=1.00 area=3.14
Total area: 105.68

test_cases.label: Hidden -- multiple circles
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Circle: r=10.00 area=314.16
Circle: r=0.50 area=0.79
Total area: 314.95

exercises.title: Animal Hierarchy with Dynamic Casting and Slicing Protection
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a polymorphic `Animal` hierarchy with an abstract base, derived `Dog` and `Cat` classes, and a `Pet` interface. Use `dynamic_cast` to detect which animals are pets and demonstrate that virtual dispatch works correctly through base pointers.

## Input

- Line 1: integer N (1 <= N <= 20), the number of animals.
- Next N lines: either `dog <name>` or `cat <name>`, where name is a single word.

## Output

For each animal in input order, print one line via polymorphic `speak()`:
- For a dog: `<name> says Woof!`
- For a cat: `<name> says Meow!`

Then print a blank line, followed by the pet report. For each animal that can be `dynamic_cast` to `Pet*`, print:
- `<name> is a pet (trick: <trick>)`

where `<trick>` is `fetch` for dogs and `purr` for cats.

Finally print: `Pets found: <count>`

## Requirements

1. Abstract class `Animal`: `std::string m_name`, constructor taking `std::string`, `virtual ~Animal() = default`, `const std::string& getName() const`, pure virtual `std::string speak() const = 0`.
2. Interface class `Pet` (no data members): `virtual ~Pet() = default`, pure virtual `std::string trick() const = 0`.
3. `Dog` inherits from both `Animal` and `Pet`. `speak()` returns `"<name> says Woof!"`. `trick()` returns `"fetch"`.
4. `Cat` inherits from both `Animal` and `Pet`. `speak()` returns `"<name> says Meow!"`. `trick()` returns `"purr"`.
5. Use `override` on every overridden function.
6. Store animals in `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Animal>>`. In the pet-detection loop, use `dynamic_cast<Pet*>(ptr.get())` to check if the animal implements `Pet`.
7. Use `<iostream>`, `<memory>`, `<vector>`, `<string>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- mixed dogs and cats
test_cases.stdin:
4
dog Rex
cat Luna
dog Max
cat Milo
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rex says Woof!
Luna says Meow!
Max says Woof!
Milo says Meow!

Rex is a pet (trick: fetch)
Luna is a pet (trick: purr)
Max is a pet (trick: fetch)
Milo is a pet (trick: purr)
Pets found: 4

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Buddy says Woof!

Buddy is a pet (trick: fetch)
Pets found: 1

test_cases.label: Hidden -- three cats
test_cases.stdin:
3
cat Whiskers
cat Shadow
cat Nala
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Whiskers says Meow!
Shadow says Meow!
Nala says Meow!

Whiskers is a pet (trick: purr)
Shadow is a pet (trick: purr)
Nala is a pet (trick: purr)
Pets found: 3
